content:
# 2 Peter Chapter 1 — Translation Disagreements
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Agents:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

## Verse 1 — "Symeon" vs. "Simeon"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "Symeon Petros" |
| LOGOS | "Symeon Peter" |
| PNEUMA | "Simeon Peter" |

**Nature:** Orthographic / transliteration choice.
**Discussion:** All three agents acknowledge the Sinaiticus reading CYMECON. EMET and LOGOS transliterate as "Symeon" (preserving the Greek upsilon); PNEUMA uses the Anglicized "Simeon." Additionally, EMET retains the Greek "Petros" while LOGOS and PNEUMA use "Peter."
**SOPHIA ruling:** "Symeon Peter" — preserves the distinctive Sinaiticus Hebraic form while using the standard English form of the surname.

---

## Verse 1 — "slave" vs. "servant"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "a slave and apostle" |
| LOGOS | "a servant and apostle" |
| PNEUMA | "a servant and apostle" |

**Nature:** Semantic range of doulos.
**Discussion:** The Greek doulos denotes one who is owned, a bondslave. EMET preserves the stark literal sense. LOGOS and PNEUMA use "servant," which is traditional but softer. Both renderings are defensible.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "servant" — adopted as majority consensus; the term in biblical context already implies total allegiance. The literal force of "slave" is noted for study purposes.

---

## Verse 3 — "by his own glory and virtue" vs. "by means of his own marvelous glory and excellence"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "by [his] own glory and virtue" |
| LOGOS | "by his own glory and virtue" |
| PNEUMA | "by means of his own marvelous glory and excellence" |

**Nature:** Degree of interpretive expansion; word choice for arete.
**Discussion:** PNEUMA adds "marvelous" (not in Greek) and substitutes "excellence" for "virtue" (arete). EMET and LOGOS stay closer to the source. The Sinaiticus reading supports "glory and virtue" without amplification.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "by his own glory and virtue" — follows EMET/LOGOS; no textual basis for "marvelous."

---

## Verse 4 — "greatest" vs. "exceedingly great" vs. "great"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "precious and greatest promises" |
| LOGOS | "precious and exceedingly great promises" |
| PNEUMA | "great and precious promises" |

**Nature:** Treatment of the superlative megista.
**Discussion:** The Greek megista is a true superlative. EMET renders literally ("greatest"), LOGOS uses the periphrastic "exceedingly great," and PNEUMA downgrades to the simple positive "great." PNEUMA's rendering loses the superlative force.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "precious and exceedingly great promises" — retains superlative force in natural English.

---

## Verse 4 — "desire" vs. "sinful desires"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "by desire" |
| LOGOS | "through desire" |
| PNEUMA | "caused by sinful desires" |

**Nature:** Interpretive addition.
**Discussion:** The Greek en epithumia ("in/by desire") does not include a modifier like "sinful." PNEUMA adds "sinful" and pluralizes for clarity. While the context implies corrupt desire, the word itself is neutral in Greek (it can mean any strong desire). EMET and LOGOS stay closer to source.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "through sinful desire" — a light interpretive addition is warranted since the context (corruption in the world) makes the negative sense clear, but singular is retained as in the Greek.

---

## Verse 5 — Virtue chain phrasing

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "supply in the faith of you the virtue, and in the virtue the knowledge" |
| LOGOS | "supply in your faith virtue, and in your virtue knowledge" |
| PNEUMA | "Add to your faith moral excellence, and to moral excellence add knowledge" |

**Nature:** Structural and lexical.
**Discussion:** PNEUMA substitutes "moral excellence" for "virtue" (arete) and uses "add to... add" for accessibility. EMET preserves the awkward Greek word order. LOGOS finds a middle path. The Greek epichoregeo means "supply abundantly" rather than simply "add."
**SOPHIA ruling:** Follows LOGOS phrasing. "Virtue" is retained over "moral excellence" for conciseness and fidelity to the single Greek word arete.

---

## Verse 8 — "belonging to you and abounding" vs. "are yours and are increasing" vs. "the more you grow like this"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "For these things belonging to you and abounding make you not idle nor unfruitful" |
| LOGOS | "For if these things are yours and are increasing, they render you neither idle nor unfruitful" |
| PNEUMA | "The more you grow like this, the more productive and useful you will be" |

**Nature:** Structural divergence; degree of paraphrase.
**Discussion:** PNEUMA completely restructures as a comparative ("the more... the more") and substitutes "productive and useful" for "not idle nor unfruitful." EMET preserves the litotes (double negative). LOGOS preserves the structure while adding a conditional "if" that is implied.
**SOPHIA ruling:** Follows LOGOS. The litotes "neither idle nor unfruitful" is preserved as it reflects the Greek negative construction.

---

## Verse 9 — "having received forgetfulness" vs. "forgetting"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "having received forgetfulness of the cleansing of the former sins of him" |
| LOGOS | "having received forgetfulness of the cleansing of his former sins" |
| PNEUMA | "forgetting that they have been cleansed from their old sins" |

**Nature:** Idiom preservation vs. dynamic rendering.
**Discussion:** The Greek lethen labon ("having taken/received forgetfulness") is a deliberate Hellenistic idiom suggesting willful forgetting. PNEUMA simplifies to "forgetting," losing the intentionality. EMET and LOGOS preserve the idiom.
**SOPHIA ruling:** Follows EMET/LOGOS: "having received forgetfulness." The idiom conveys theological weight — this is not mere absentmindedness.

---

## Verse 10 — "brothers" vs. "brothers and sisters"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "brothers" |
| LOGOS | "brothers" |
| PNEUMA | "brothers and sisters" |

**Nature:** Gender-inclusive language.
**Discussion:** The Greek adelphoi is grammatically masculine but functionally inclusive in Koine Greek, addressing the whole community. PNEUMA makes this explicit; EMET and LOGOS retain the traditional rendering.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "brothers" — retains the form of the Greek. A footnote in a study edition could note the inclusive address.

---

## Verse 13 — "tent" vs. "body"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "in this tent" |
| LOGOS | "in this tent" |
| PNEUMA | "in this body" |

**Nature:** Metaphor preservation vs. interpretation.
**Discussion:** The Greek skenoma ("tent, tabernacle") is a deliberate metaphor for the mortal body as a temporary dwelling. PNEUMA interprets the metaphor directly. EMET and LOGOS preserve it.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "in this tent" — the metaphor is theologically rich (cf. 2 Cor 5:1-4) and well understood in biblical context.

---

## Verse 14 — "swift" vs. "soon"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "swift is the putting off of the tent of me" |
| LOGOS | "the putting off of my tent is soon" |
| PNEUMA | "I must soon leave this earthly life" |

**Nature:** Nuance of tachine; degree of paraphrase.
**Discussion:** The Greek tachine can mean "swift" (manner) or "imminent/soon" (time). EMET reads "swift" (describing the manner of death), LOGOS reads "soon" (temporal), and PNEUMA paraphrases extensively. Both temporal and manner senses are possible.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "soon" — the temporal sense fits the context of Peter writing near the end of his life. PNEUMA's full paraphrase departs too far from source.

---

## Verse 16 — "that one's majesty" vs. "his majesty" vs. "his majestic splendor"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "eyewitnesses of the majesty of that one" |
| LOGOS | "eyewitnesses of that one's majesty" |
| PNEUMA | "We saw his majestic splendor with our own eyes" |

**Nature:** Demonstrative pronoun rendering; degree of expansion.
**Discussion:** The Greek ekeinou ("of that one") is a demonstrative used with deliberate distance/reverence. PNEUMA collapses it to "his" and adds "with our own eyes" (redundant with "eyewitnesses"). EMET and LOGOS preserve the demonstrative.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "eyewitnesses of his majesty" — uses "his" for readability while preserving the structure. The reverential distance of ekeinou is noted but "that one's" reads awkwardly in English.

---

## Verse 17 — Divine voice wording

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "This is the Son of me, the Beloved, in whom I was well pleased" |
| LOGOS | "This is my Son, my Beloved, in whom I am well pleased" |
| PNEUMA | "This is my dearly loved Son, who brings me great joy" |

**Nature:** Tense of eudokesa; degree of paraphrase.
**Discussion:** The Greek aorist eudokesa is rendered as past ("was well pleased") by EMET and present ("am well pleased") by LOGOS. PNEUMA departs significantly with "brings me great joy." The aorist can function as a gnomic/timeless statement. Additionally, EMET preserves "the Beloved" (ho agapetos) as a title, while PNEUMA converts to an adjective phrase.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "This is my Son, my Beloved, in whom I am well pleased" — follows LOGOS. The present tense captures the timeless quality of divine pleasure. "My Beloved" preserves the titular sense.

---

## Verse 18 — "brought from heaven" vs. "borne from heaven" vs. "from heaven"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "having been brought from heaven" |
| LOGOS | "heard borne from heaven" |
| PNEUMA | "heard that voice from heaven" |

**Nature:** Verb choice for enechtheisan; simplification.
**Discussion:** The Greek enechtheisan (aorist passive of phero, "to bear/carry") describes the voice as "having been carried/borne." PNEUMA drops this verb entirely. EMET and LOGOS preserve it with slight variation ("brought" vs. "borne").
**SOPHIA ruling:** "borne from heaven" — "borne" captures the passive voice and the sense of divine origin.

---

## Verse 19 — "more firm" vs. "more sure" vs. "even greater confidence"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "the prophetic word made more firm" |
| LOGOS | "the prophetic word made more sure" |
| PNEUMA | "even greater confidence in the message proclaimed by the prophets" |

**Nature:** Comparative adjective target; structural reframing.
**Discussion:** EMET and LOGOS apply the comparative bebaioteron to the prophetic word itself (it is made more firm/sure). PNEUMA shifts the comparison to the believer's confidence, which changes the theological emphasis. The Greek syntax modifies the word, not the person's confidence.
**SOPHIA ruling:** "the prophetic word made more sure" — follows LOGOS. The comparative modifies the word itself.

---

## Verse 20 — "does not come to be of one's own interpretation" vs. "comes about by" vs. "ever came from the prophet's own understanding"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "every prophecy of Scripture does not come to be of one's own interpretation" |
| LOGOS | "no prophecy of Scripture comes about by one's own interpretation" |
| PNEUMA | "no prophecy in Scripture ever came from the prophet's own understanding" |

**Nature:** Negation placement; interpretive specificity.
**Discussion:** EMET places negation after subject ("every... does not"), which is syntactically faithful to the Greek (pasa... ou) but awkward in English. LOGOS restructures to "no prophecy" for clarity. PNEUMA adds "the prophet's" (not in Greek, though contextually implied by v.21) and substitutes "understanding" for "interpretation" (epilusis).
**SOPHIA ruling:** Follows LOGOS: "no prophecy of Scripture comes about by one's own interpretation." Clear English while preserving ambiguity of "one's own."

---

## Verse 21 — Word order and "holy" before "men"

| Agent | Rendering |
|-------|-----------|
| EMET | "being carried by the Holy Spirit, men spoke from God" |
| LOGOS | "men spoke from God, being carried along by the Holy Spirit" |
| PNEUMA | "people spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit" |

**Nature:** Clause order; "men" vs. "people"; Sinaiticus variant (omission of hagioi).
**Discussion:** Sinaiticus omits hagioi ("holy") before anthropoi ("men"), which all three agents correctly follow. EMET preserves the Greek clause order (Spirit-clause first); LOGOS and PNEUMA place the main clause first. PNEUMA uses gender-neutral "people."
**SOPHIA ruling:** "men spoke from God, being carried along by the Holy Spirit" — follows LOGOS clause order for English readability. "Men" retained as the Greek anthropoi is the word used.

---

## Summary of Disagreements

| Verse | Category | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|-------|----------|------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | Name form | Symeon Petros | Symeon Peter | Simeon Peter | Symeon Peter |
| 1 | doulos | slave | servant | servant | servant |
| 3 | arete + expansion | glory and virtue | glory and virtue | marvelous glory and excellence | glory and virtue |
| 4 | megista | greatest | exceedingly great | great | exceedingly great |
| 4 | epithumia | by desire | through desire | sinful desires | through sinful desire |
| 5 | arete | virtue | virtue | moral excellence | virtue |
| 8 | Structure | literal litotes | formal litotes | comparative paraphrase | formal litotes |
| 9 | lethen labon | having received forgetfulness | having received forgetfulness | forgetting | having received forgetfulness |
| 10 | adelphoi | brothers | brothers | brothers and sisters | brothers |
| 13 | skenoma | tent | tent | body | tent |
| 14 | tachine | swift | soon | soon (paraphrase) | soon |
| 16 | ekeinou | of that one | that one's | his (expanded) | his |
| 17 | Voice wording | was well pleased | am well pleased | brings me great joy | am well pleased |
| 18 | phero passive | brought | borne | (omitted) | borne |
| 19 | bebaioteron | more firm | more sure | greater confidence | more sure |
| 20 | Negation / epilusis | every...does not / interpretation | no...by / interpretation | no...from / understanding | no...by / interpretation |
| 21 | Clause order | Spirit-clause first | main clause first | main clause first | main clause first |

**Total disagreement points logged: 17**

-- SOPHIA, Consensus Arbiter
